title: Modern Park Bench
summary: A six-foot park-style bench with arms and a backrest, framed in 2x4 and decked in 1x6 — glue at every joint, because a public-duty bench earns it.
description:
Park benches are a specific brief: long enough for three, arms to push up from, a back angled for sitting rather than slouching, and construction that shrugs off years outside. This design hits it with seven 2x4s and six 1x6s — a rectangular seat frame with five crosspieces (that's what keeps a 6-foot span from ever bouncing), leg assemblies that carry arms into taller back legs, and stretchers low down to stop splay.

The plan's opening line is the whole quality philosophy: wood glue at ALL joints. Screws clamp; glue is what makes a hundred small joints act like one stiff frame after five years of freeze-thaw. Use an exterior glue and don't skip a joint because it's 'just a stretcher.'

The screw schedule is deliberate too — three per joint where the legs meet the seat frame (the highest-leverage connection), two most other places. Follow it rather than averaging it out. Deck boards go on last with the shorter screws, and the sander earns its keep on the arm tops, which are the part everyone actually touches.

tools.note: Arm tops and seat front edge — the touch surfaces.

materials.name: Exterior screws, #9 x 2-1/2" to 3", self-tapping, star drive
Exterior screws, #8 x 1-1/2" to 2", self-tapping, star drive
Exterior wood glue
materials.note: At ALL joints — the plan's first instruction, and the right one.

steps.title: Build the seat frame
steps.body: Assemble the two 72" rails with the five 21" crosspieces — glue every joint, two of the longer screws per joint. Five crosspieces on a six-foot bench is the anti-sag budget; space them evenly.
materials: Exterior screws, #9 x 2-1/2" to 3", self-tapping, star drive

steps.title: Add the legs — three screws each
steps.body: Attach the front and back legs to the seat frame with glue and THREE screws per joint. This is the bench's highest-leverage connection — every sit levers the legs against these screws, which is why this joint alone gets the third screw.
materials: Exterior screws, #9 x 2-1/2" to 3", self-tapping, star drive

steps.title: Land the arms
steps.body: Set each arm on top of its front leg and tie it into the taller back leg — glue, two screws per joint. Check the arms are level with each other; on a bench, mismatched arms read instantly.
materials: Exterior screws, #9 x 2-1/2" to 3", self-tapping, star drive

steps.title: Add the leg stretchers
steps.body: Screw the short stretchers between the bottom of each leg pair, glue and two screws per joint. Their job is exactly what the plan says: they stop the legs splaying under load.
materials: Exterior screws, #9 x 2-1/2" to 3", self-tapping, star drive

steps.title: Run the bottom stretcher
steps.body: Center the 69" stretcher between the two leg assemblies, low, and attach with two screws per end. It ties the ends into one frame — the difference between a bench and two chairs holding hands.
materials: Exterior screws, #9 x 2-1/2" to 3", self-tapping, star drive

steps.title: Mount the backrest supports
steps.body: Attach the three backrest supports with glue and two screws per joint — ends and center. These set the back's angle; dry-fit a back board and sit against it before the glue grabs if you want to fine-tune the lean.
materials: Exterior screws, #9 x 2-1/2" to 3", self-tapping, star drive

steps.title: Deck it and finish
steps.body:
Lay the 1x6 seat and back boards with even gaps and screw down with the shorter screws, two per joint. Sand the arm tops and the seat's front edge until they're kind to skin, then finish exterior-grade.

Place it where the afternoon light lands, and accept that it will become the most-photographed thing in the yard.
materials: Exterior screws, #8 x 1-1/2" to 2", self-tapping, star drive

images.alt: Six-foot modern wooden park bench with arms and slatted back
